speaker: interviewer
text: May 29, 1996. Mette Shayne, maiden name Hannover. My name is Peggy Marc Kaz. We're in Chicago, Illinois, USA. The language of the interview will be English. Today is Wednesday, May 29, 1996. My name is Peggy Marc Kaz and I will be interviewing Mette Shayne in Chicago, Illinois, USA. We'll be speaking English.

speaker: interviewer
text: The mirror is very useful. Please tell me your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mette Shayne, M-E-T-T-E S-H-A-Y-N-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hannover.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-A-N-N-O-V-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born October 9, 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Copenhagen, Denmark.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to start out in the way distant past, because you have in your possession something that very few people have. You have a genealogy that goes back about 300 years. Can you tell us about that, a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the first known in my family to come to Denmark came in 1683, was one of the first Jews that came to Denmark and was allowed to, to settle and, and work in Denmark. And, and all of my family seemed to have a, a, a genealogy that goes back that far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the family come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came from Germany. That time--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they live in Copenhagen the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This person settled in Copenhagen. I think some came up through Jutland, and so they-- but they eventually ended up in Copenhagen. And by far, most of the Jews in Denmark live in Copenhagen. But there aren't so many.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family participate in any particular segments of Danish life? Did they have particular occupations or professions?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean way back? I think they were merchants, and later on became academicians as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's fast forward to your lifetime. You were born in 1934. Tell me about your early childhood. What do you remember about your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember so much before, before the war, except some family life in general. It was a close family, the, the extended family. And I have one brother. But, so the first things that I remember is from going to school and living in the house that we lived in was built when I was born. So it had affectionate relation to the-- and then the first I remember as far as the war was concerned was in April 9, 1940, when the German, Germans invaded Denmark. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- excuse me. Let's talk a little bit more about your early childhood. Tell me about who lived in the house with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my, my-- in the house was my brother and my, and my parents. And we had, at that time, one had maids, and we had two maids living with us. And I had a maid that took me to school on a bicycle in the morning. And, and I'm still in contact with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your house located? Was it in a suburb?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the suburb, but just, just on the border of Copenhagen. The, the end of the garden was, was Copenhagen. So it was very close into the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was president of a electric manufacturing company, like a very small G for Denmark.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did he happen to get involved in that company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he is from a family of engineers. His father was, was an engineer, and, and president of the-- the what, what is the MIT of Denmark. And his brothers were all engineers. And he actually had been in this country to get further training and was called back to, to this directorship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived a comfortable life, a good life?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived a comfortable life, without being extravagant in many ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me something very interesting about your background. Your family was assimilated.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was very assimilated, and I did not know I was Jewish until 1943, when we had to hide and, and flee in the country. We did not celebrate any Jewish holidays, and I had, had no knowledge of it at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your father would occasionally go to synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I know that he, he would go on Rosh-- on Yom Kippur. And actually he had inherited from his father a seat in the synagogue. And I believe my grandfather also would go every year on, on Yom Kippur. But, but I didn't know about that before the war. But I do know that after the war, he would try to go if he was in the country at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we get to April of 1940. The Germans invaded. Tell me what you remember of that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all I remember is seeing the planes come and dropping leaflets to say that, that, that they were friendly planes. And life pretty much continued for us children uninterrupted. And slowly, as the confrontation started with, with the, the people who were, were working against the Germans, young people like us also would demonstrate some defiance by wearing the Royal Air Force colors and things like that. But on the other hand, one had to be very careful, you know, not to make too much trouble, unless one was going to be arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so those are the kind of defiant things that I remember, and just knowing that we didn't like the Germans, and, you know, were scared when the German soldiers very often were, were stationed around where we lived, because of the-- because it was a main highway. Now, you were six at the time of the invasion, or just prior to six. Do you remember your parents talking about the war ever, or your friends talking about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We followed the war with-- and, and listened to the BBC, because the news in Denmark, of course, by then became controlled by the Germans. But I had no knowledge of what that meant, as far as Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked my mother recently, you know, how could they have been so naive? And, and they said, they did know something about what was going on in Germany, but they kept thinking this could never happen in Denmark, just like the German Jews never thought it could happen in Germany. Did you have Jewish playmates that you know of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. My, my, my best friend was not Jewish. And I just-- when all this came up again, asked her, because I'm in correspondence with her, did she know? And she told me that she did know, because she, she was from a quite religious family, and her father had explained to her about it, which was interesting, because I didn't know, but she knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in those early days of the war, when you had German soldiers near your house, and you committed small acts of defiance by wearing RAF colors and things like that, what was the sense of-- do you remember what, what sense you had of what was to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, I had no sense of it at all, since I didn't even know I was Jewish, and I didn't know there was a Jewish problem. I just-- what we were worried about was the Germans and whether the, the Ally would, would win the war. And we followed that. And we, of course, felt the war both because of food rationing and, and having to black, black out windows and having curfews at night. So those were the kind of things that we, that we felt as far as the war was concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the fighting never really came close to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fighting, there were some bombardment, in '43. It kind of became more, more obvious. And I remember in 1943 going to dancing school, and on the way home, having to go into a, a, a shelter, because there was a bombardment. And when we came out from the shelter, there were huge fires, and they had bombarded a sugar factory that was going on fire. So, so that you could see the-- and, and several times we had to go down in, in our shelter in, in the house, that was built in the house, when there were bombardments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father's business affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's business was affected. He, he was under a lot of pressure to produce for the Germans, which he refused to do. And they did have, at some point, German guards in the factory. He also was approached by the Germans, saying that if he would work for them, they would make sure that he would be saved if there was a action against the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew that your father was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they-- and they made representations to him that he would be safe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. But needless to say, he, he did not go along with that. And wouldn't have been saved anyway. So it was useless. But he did know a lot of people in the, in the higher echelons in the industry and in the, in the foreign ministry. And that is where he, he dealt with a lot of people there, because of his company exporting. And he would-- those last months was in constant contact with them to find out if there was anything about the rumors that by then had started going, that, that there would be action against the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now again, I want to return to this question of the concern that you had at home that something would happen. I believe you told me your mother didn't think that Jews would be taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother was not brought up with anything, as far as Jewish religion. And she was extremely assimilated and, and just couldn't believe that this would happen. And even after we had fled from Copenhagen, she refused to leave Denmark until she heard that actually the action had taken place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go into that. [COUGH] Excuse me. That first week of October, 1943, when you had to leave. Tell me about the events that led up to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it started about the, the 26th of September. It was the first night that my father came home and said that we would have to pack up some things and, and, and leave the house and not stay in the house at night because of the rumors that were going on. So that was a time that they explained to us that we were Jewish and, and tried to explain to us what that meant and why, why we had to leave our house. And that night we slept at, at a neighbor's house, in the basement. But nothing happened that night. And the next day we went back and went back to school. And the 27th, that night we slept at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you, you were nine years old when this, when this took place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was just before I-- my ninth birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as a nine-year-old child, how did-- how did you perceive all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember much more than the fact that, you know, it was scary and, and I don't think I got a very good understanding of, of what the difference was between me and my friends at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about it in school? Did you tell your friends at school that you may have to leave, or there was some talk about having to evacuate?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think there was any talk about it until when, when we were gone, and then our friends were explained by their, their parents what had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had this talk with your parents. They sat you down and they said we have to leave, because we're Jews and it's not safe anymore. And you went and spent the night, and then came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then spent another day in our, in our own house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the following day, now we up to the 28th. I tell you, the 29th I remember. The 28th, again, we, we slept at our friend's house, but again went back to school the following day. The 29th was the king's birthday, and my father's birthday. And usually there was-- we would go in and celebrate the king at the castle. But because of the situation in the country, there was no celebration for the king. And, and I believe that was the night of Rosh Hashanah. Now I get a little muddled. I think that-- yeah, this-- yeah, this, the 29th, again, we did not sleep at home. And the 30th, we left. We left Copenhagen. And that was the, I believe that was the night of Rosh Hashanah, where, where the Jewish community was being warned, and the rabbi in temple told everybody that there would be no service, that they should all leave and tell everybody they, they could tell to also leave. And, and that there would be an action very shortly after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, those nights that you spent away from home, who did you stay with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed with neighbors. We lived, we lived on a, on a main road, but behind us was a small, private road with, with houses. And these houses were all friends who lived in them. And my father had made, had had some small ladders made that-- so that the-- grown-ups could climb over the ladders at night when there was curfew and spend the evenings together. So there was a real network in this little enclave. And these were friends, and we stayed with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the arrangement that had been made was on the basis that you were Jews who would have to escape. Your neighbors--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not-- the, the ladders had nothing to do with that. That was, had to do with the curfew in general, and that if they wanted to see each other, you know, and get together socially at night, this was the only way they could do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then as far as the hiding you, sheltering you for those couple of nights?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were, were friends who were not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Danish community in general went to great lengths to protect the Jewish community. Did friends of yours, did you know people who had similar experiences with their neighbors, who-- people who were sheltered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mean, the-- I know all my, my family and friends and everybody was sheltering somebody. And a lot of people knew people who had summer houses, and they fled out to the country and stayed in, in their friends' summer houses out in the country. So everybody was opening their, their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we come to the night of Rosh Hashanah, about the 28th--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or 29th of September. And people were given warnings that the action was going to take place. Tell me a little bit more about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there had been-- the communication had been between the, the head of the Jewish community and, and-- who had constantly been in, in touch with the ministry and had kept telling them nothing was happening. But on that day, I believe it was, there was a German man who was involved with the Ministry of Commerce, and he went to the ministry and told them that this was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was really a German who, who saved us in that respect, that he, he went and said this is happening. You can't close your eyes for it anymore. And it's going to happen such and such a day. And he was, he came back to Denmark after the war and was, was properly thanked for his-- I, I think he was a consul or something in Denmark after the war. And he partially saved our lives. So the announcement was made, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody, just about everybody, went into hiding, and very fast. And there was a lot of bicycling around town to, to, to warn people, because of course you didn't want to talk on the phone. Nothing was, was safe in that respect. So everybody was bicycling around town, telling everybody else to hide. And in our family, my father and his brothers kind of divided who would take care of who. So some people were more able to take care of themselves than others. And my father kind of took in charge one uncle and his, and his wife, because they were more nervous people, that he felt that they would need help. And so they, they came with us and fled with us. So that the day that we finally, when we left Copenhagen, which was on the, on the 30th of September, my aunt and uncle and their two children came along with us on, on a train, which we--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Gunner ?] and Kis Markus. And they actually, my, my uncle had a very good friend who had already been active in the, in the opposition and, and among the freedom fighters, and he accompanied us and tried to help us make arrangements to find a boat at that time. And he was with us on the train down to, to the part of Denmark that where we fled from, which was the southern part of Denmark, not very conveniently located to Sweden, but they thought because it wasn't, that the Germans wouldn't think of it and it would be safer. Most people fled directly across the, the sound between Denmark and Sweden, which at one point was only a 20-minute boat ride, and at best, like, you know, maybe an hour, an hour and a half. Where we were was considerably bigger distance. And so when it didn't go smoothly, it was a very bad way to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we left-- I mean, during the day. We took the train down during the day to the southern island, where we, where we were waiting that-- and that night were told that, that they were not able to arrange for a boat that night. And as the evening went on, I believe this friend of my uncle's knew a farmer, and they took us out to this farm where we stayed overnight that night. And on this farm, they were going to have the big hunt of the year was starting the next day, so they were having a lot of company.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I vividly remember sleeping in the same bed as my cousin and my parents, and my aunt and uncle slept in the same room, and, and each-- with the two of them in twin beds. It was not quite the way they were used, used to. But we were lucky that somebody was nice enough to have us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the next day, we again were picked up by cabs and taken to a forest, where we were promised there would be a boat at night. But, and we waited in this forest near the water all evening. And then no boat materialized. And at the end, there was a children's summer camp, and they broke into the camp and put us down on the springs and the beds and covered us up with their coats, so the grown-ups were freezing all night, distraught about not hearing any boat. And the next day, we then were picked up again. And, and all of these pick-ups, by then we were-- a lot of other people had joined us, and we were probably 20. The other people were not people we knew. But these pick-ups of cabs coming and going out in the country was not exactly something that, that was normal. So it was a very dangerous situation, and you never knew whether the cab drivers could be trusted or not. But we were brought back into a city, and we stayed in a hotel for a couple of hours. And they brought food up to the rooms. They didn't think we should sit in the restaurant and be seen. And at this point, this friend of my uncle's came and announced that he was buying a boat for my father to sail it, because he couldn't find anybody who could do it. And my mother said that we might as well go to Germany, because there was no way my father could sail a boat. He knew nothing about boats. And she refused. And then a little later, somebody came and said they had a boat that night. And we were ordered to walk out to this very landing, which was about a five-mile walk from the city. And that I still can see very clearly. I can see us walking down this country road with our small suitcases. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of day was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the middle of the afternoon. I mean, it was so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were 20 of you, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we walked, we walked some miles apart. But still, it was not a very usual suit. We also-- a usual sight. We also had a lot of clothes on, because my mother had put, you know, two sets of coats and two sets of everything, because it was the only way we could bring something along. So we were very warm at this point. And we came out to this beach, where we, where we hid in some bushes. And my father had been designated to collect the money that, that was asked for, for, for the transported across. So he went on to meet with the skipper, and we were lying there in the bushes until it got dark. And meanwhile, my father had expressed to the people who had arranged the boat his worry about my cousin, who was three years younger than me, that he wasn't going to be able to walk this way. And these people very nicely rode their bikes back and picked up my cousin, my two cousins, and brought them out on the bikes. And they joined us down on the beach where we were. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that night, a boat did appear. And we knew very little about anything, except that this, this boat was coming. And we were just told to be very quiet. And when we were given the word to get on board, and it started to rain, and we were given the word, we all got on board. And there was a lot of people. We ended up being 24 people on this little fishing boat which was meant for three. And everybody just did what they were told and got down very quietly, and the boat took off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was, of course, at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was at night, yeah. It started about 7 o'clock at night. By then, it's dark in Denmark in October already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had several people who helped you along the way. You had the cooperation of local people. They must have known you were Jews. Otherwise, you wouldn't have been fleeing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At that point, all of these people obviously knew what was going on. Yes. And the fishermen were just fabulous. One of the men especially-- my brother remembers this better than I-- they called him the Lemon, and-- in Danish, and he was one of the well-known freedom fighters. And he was taken by the Germans and shot. And his grave is in the memorial park for-- for those who died during the war, which is close to my home in, in the suburb of Copenhagen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was one of the people that helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember meeting him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, no. But I, I just remember there were some men there who were very helpful, and, you know. But obviously they, they and the fishermen all put their lives in danger to help us. He was not taken at that time. He was taken later, on another--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there you were, at about 7:00 at night, around the 1st of October.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was the 2nd of October.

speaker: interviewer
text: 2nd of October.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mm-hmm. And by then, we had heard on the radio-- from the farm, actually, is when we heard-- that the action had taken place and that the Jews had been rounded up. But because of, of the warnings, only about 400 Jews were, were captured in, in Denmark and, and were sent to Theresienstadt. And out of those, almost all came back home. I think less than 30 died in the process, which is a remarkable figure compared to, to any other country. And some of those were old people. I knew one of them, a very old woman, who probably would not have lived anyway. So it was very fortunate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember listening to that radio broadcast?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember that. At the farm, we were preparing for this hunting lodge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was it reported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all I remember is that they said that there had been this action.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was state-controlled, Nazi-controlled radio? Or this was an underground radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think this must have been the state-controlled radio, and it-- and that the announcement was just very brief and taut. And what we learned later was that there had been a-- what was called a razia, a, a, that, that the Germans had come to the hotel where we had stayed, two hours after we left, because they had been tipped off that there were Jews there. And so we, again, we were very lucky that we had gotten out when we got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: The hotel in the-- in the small town in the south?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we got out just by the-- because as I said, even if, if we supposedly were trying to be very careful, and we're seeing all these people walking down the country road with suitcases, it's not exactly very well hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think we'll stop now, and then we'll pick up the story after the tape change.
